notes.note: Pompey, here named only obliquely. Cicero describes his ally's sudden fall from popularity since allying with Caesar: "swimming in glory," he is now "broken in spirit" and friendless, hated by good and bad alike.

notes.anchor: as Apelles, if he had seen his Venus, or Protogenes his Ialysus
notes.note: Apelles (his Venus Anadyomene) and Protogenes (his Ialysus) were the two most celebrated painters of antiquity. Cicero casts himself as a painter whose own masterpiece, the figure of Pompey "painted and polished with all the colours of my art," he must now watch "smeared with mud."

Archilochus of Paros was the archetype of savage iambic invective. Bibulus's edicts mocking Pompey and Caesar are "Archilochian" in their lacerating venom; crowds gathered to read them where they were posted.

notes.anchor: what you have done with the Sicyonians
notes.note: Sicyon, a city of the Peloponnese, owed money to Atticus; recovering the debt was a long-running and vexing piece of his business that Cicero repeatedly asks after. The matter recurs across the Atticus letters of these years.
